questions.prompt: Why store auth tokens in HttpOnly cookies instead of localStorage?

choices.text: localStorage is readable from JavaScript — any XSS vulnerability anywhere in your site can read tokens and exfiltrate them. HttpOnly cookies are explicitly hidden from JavaScript (`document.cookie` returns nothing for HttpOnly cookies); only the browser includes them in outgoing requests automatically. So if XSS happens, with HttpOnly: attacker can make requests AS the user from within the current page but can't steal the token for use elsewhere; with localStorage: attacker grabs the token, exfiltrates it, uses it from any device until expiry. Trade-off: HttpOnly cookies require CSRF defense (SameSite=Lax/Strict + CSRF tokens for state-changing requests). The net is much better than localStorage tokens. XSS happens; design assuming it does.

questions.explanation: HttpOnly hides cookies from JS. XSS can't exfiltrate them. localStorage tokens get stolen on any XSS.

questions.prompt: Your bank app lets users transfer money via POST /transfer with cookie auth. Defenses needed against CSRF?

choices.text: Layered defense: (1) Set the session cookie with `SameSite=Lax` (default in modern browsers) or `SameSite=Strict` — prevents the browser from sending the cookie on cross-origin POSTs from attacker.com, defeating most CSRF, (2) Add CSRF tokens — random per-session value the form must include; attacker doesn't know it (it's not sent on cross-origin requests), so forged requests miss it; most frameworks (Django, Rails) generate this automatically, (3) Re-authentication for sensitive actions like money transfer — require entering password even within authenticated session, so even a successful CSRF can't transfer without the password, (4) Audit logging — every transfer logged with IP / device / user-agent so anomalies are visible. SameSite alone catches most modern CSRF; tokens + reauth are belt-and-suspenders. The combination is what banks actually run.

questions.explanation: SameSite cookies + CSRF tokens + reauth for sensitive actions + audit logging. Layered defense.
